summary: A full liberation-centered Haggadah that joins traditional structure, gender-expansive language, accessible explanation, and social-justice interpretation; its licensed text should be included as a substantial source, with separate gates for land refrains, dated examples, and embedded authors.

sourceAssessment.voice: Expansive, justice-centered, gender-conscious, liturgically engaged, and often conversational, with Hebrew, transliteration, historical notes, songs, and contemporary readings.
bestUses: A liberation or LGBTQ-inclusive backbone built from complete source-order movements
Clear Maggid, Four Children, plague, Miriam, and symbol sequences with a distinctive authorial voice
Meal and peace passages that maintain the traditional order while broadening participation
beginnerRisks: The source is long and alternates among liturgy, commentary, songs, and appendices; extracts need visual and genre checks.
Some inherited land language and a final Jerusalem refrain are allowed only under the project's narrow traditional rule.
Several poems, songs, statistics, and contributed readings need separate credit, rights, or fact review.
sourceAssessment.politicalContext: The source frames Passover as liberation and includes Jewish, feminist, queer, labor, and Palestinian references alongside traditional land language. Default use must remain peace-focused and non-exclusive; modern conflict passages and Jerusalem or Israel refrains require explicit editorial gates rather than blanket exclusion of the source.
sourceAssessment.creditContext: Credit Emily Aviva Kapor-Mater and identify Haggadah Shir Ge'ulah / The Song of Liberation Haggadah under CC BY 4.0, link the license, and identify modifications. Preserve the source's printed credits and conduct item-level review for embedded authors, songs, and readings.

recommendedSequences.title: Why liberation is told
recommendedSequences.beginnerContext: Introduce the Haggadah as a guide to a participatory Passover meal and explain that the story moves between ancient slavery and present responsibility. Invite guests to take turns reading and allow anyone to pass.
recommendedSequences.rationale: The early source-order records establish the edition's liberation purpose and participatory method, giving later justice readings a clear authorial frame rather than presenting them as isolated inserts.
recommendedSequences.creditNotes: Credit Emily Aviva Kapor-Mater and the CC BY 4.0 edition; indicate any abridgment.

recommendedSequences.title: Questions opening the telling
recommendedSequences.beginnerContext: Explain that asking questions is a ritual act and that Maggid is the storytelling section. Give the Four Questions a clear reader cue, then say in plain language why the group turns from questions to the Exodus story.
recommendedSequences.rationale: These adjacent records retain the question-and-response movement that launches Maggid without importing the preceding modern Israel refrain, so the sequence works in the default peace-focused edition.
recommendedSequences.creditNotes: Credit Kapor-Mater and preserve any source-presented attribution for translations or songs.

recommendedSequences.title: Four children and four questions
recommendedSequences.beginnerContext: Introduce the figures as four ways people may approach a difficult story, not labels for actual children. Translate unfamiliar terms, make reading roles optional, and give the table one strong follow-up question rather than asking guests to classify themselves.
recommendedSequences.rationale: Seven consecutive records preserve the source's full reinterpretive arc and its internal balance; using the whole movement avoids a harsh or confusing child label appearing without response.
recommendedSequences.creditNotes: Credit Kapor-Mater and retain any separately named contributor associated with the Four Children interpretation.

recommendedSequences.title: Oppression, departure, and diminished joy
recommendedSequences.beginnerContext: State the story arc before reading: the Israelites were enslaved, Moses confronted Pharaoh, plagues struck Egypt, and the people left. Explain the cup-diminishing action and say that freedom is not celebrated by ignoring Egyptian suffering.
recommendedSequences.rationale: Five adjacent passages provide a continuous Exodus-and-plagues movement with ritual response, giving novices enough narrative to understand the action and its moral complexity.
recommendedSequences.creditNotes: Credit Kapor-Mater under CC BY 4.0 and preserve any credited plague interpretation.

recommendedSequences.title: Crossing, Miriam, and gratitude
recommendedSequences.beginnerContext: Explain that the story reaches the sea, where escape becomes possible and Miriam leads song. Give guests a singing or spoken-response option, then introduce Dayenu as a gratitude chain rather than a celebration of anyone's destruction.
recommendedSequences.rationale: The five contiguous records preserve the narrative turn from danger through crossing and women's leadership into gratitude, a cohesive source-led emotional arc.
recommendedSequences.creditNotes: Credit Kapor-Mater and preserve printed song, translation, and Miriam attributions.

recommendedSequences.title: The three symbols and every generation
recommendedSequences.beginnerContext: Point to each item and define it before the reading. Explain that the three symbols carry the story into the body and that 'every generation' asks what freedom requires now.
recommendedSequences.rationale: Six consecutive records contain the complete symbol-and-memory unit, allowing the source's liberation framing to grow directly out of ritual objects rather than arrive as an unrelated theme paragraph.
recommendedSequences.creditNotes: Credit Kapor-Mater and retain any source-presented attribution attached to the interpretations.

recommendedSequences.title: Matzah, sandwich, meal, and afikoman
recommendedSequences.beginnerContext: Move one action at a time: bless and eat matzah, explain the bitter herb and haroset before building the sandwich, eat the meal, then retrieve and share the afikoman. Define every food and include allergy alternatives; supply the missing maror action from a separately verified source module.
recommendedSequences.rationale: Six source-order records preserve the usable table sequence from matzah through sandwich, meal, and afikoman while deliberately omitting a run-together OCR record that cannot yet provide exact maror text.
recommendedSequences.creditNotes: Credit Emily Aviva Kapor-Mater and preserve CC BY 4.0 attribution and change notice.

recommendedSequences.title: Gratitude turning toward peace
recommendedSequences.beginnerContext: Explain that after the meal the table gives thanks and widens the wish for wellbeing to everyone. Identify responsive lines, make prayer language optional for mixed-belief groups, and end with a concrete wish for peace and shared dignity.
recommendedSequences.rationale: The six source-order records build a coherent post-meal movement from gratitude through collective care into peace, fitting the project's required framing without an isolated political slogan.
recommendedSequences.creditNotes: Credit Kapor-Mater and preserve the attribution of any embedded prayer, poem, or translation.

reasons: The modern Israel destination line is limited to an explicit traditional selection and cannot enter the generic candidate pool.
The land refrain requires the project's traditional-without-social-justice gate.
The farmworker and tomato estimate is dated and requires current verification.
The embedded reading requires item-level rights and attribution review before runtime use.
The contributed or embedded text needs separate creator, rights, and context verification.
This reading cannot enter the approved pool until its item-level credit and reuse status are recorded.
The historically or politically difficult embedded material requires adult context and rights review.
The embedded work requires separate permission or license verification and full attribution.
The Palestine-focused poem requires its own rights, attribution, date, and editorial-context review; it is not rejected merely for its subject.
The Jerusalem closing refrain is allowed only for a traditional selection without a social-justice override.

notes: This source's CC BY 4.0 license explicitly supports reuse with attribution; it should be treated as a major source, not a marginal or excluded one.
A liberation or LGBTQ-inclusive version should draw several complete source-order movements from this Haggadah so its voice remains coherent.
Embedded works remain separately reviewable even though the containing Haggadah is licensed for reuse.
These recommendations are local-agent-reviewed candidates only and do not grant runtime eligibility.
